So the basic idea about attention um, is that we encode uh each token in the sequence into a vector
um, or so we have input, an input sequence that we'd like to
encode over and we perform a linear combination of the vectors uh, weighted by attention weights
and there's two varieties of attention uh, they're good to know about. The first one is cross
attention. Uh, where each element in a sequence attends to elements of another sequence.
And this is widely used in encoder decoder models uh, where you have one encoder and you have a
separate decoder um, these models, the popular models that are like this that people still use a
lot. Our T5 uh, is an example of an encoder decoder model or uh, M-barc is another example of
encoder decoder model. Um, but basically the uh, the way cross attention works is we have for
example in English uh, sentence here and we want to translate it into uh, into a Japanese sentence
and so when we output the first word, we would mostly uh, upweight this, or sorry, we have a,
we have a Japanese sentence and we would like to translate it into an English sentence for example.
So when we generate the first word uh, coordinate in Japanese means this. So in order to output the
first word, we would first uh, do a weighted sum of all of the embeddings of the Japanese sentence
and we would focus probably most on this word up here uh, coordinate because it corresponds to the
word this. In the next step of generating an output, uh, we would uh, attempt to different words
because different words correspond to is so you would attempt to desu which course is
um, when you output and actually there's no word in the Japanese sentence that corresponds to
answer, you might get a very like blob like uh, in a tension weight that doesn't look very uh,
that looks very smooth and that very spiky and then when you do example, you'd have strong
attention on uh, on the word that corresponds to example. There's also self-attention
and um, self-attention basically what it does is each element in a sequence attends to
elements of the same sequence and so this is a good way of doing sequence encoding just like we
used RNNs, ByRNs, uh, convolutional neural networks and so um, the reason why you would want to do
something like this, just give an example, let's say we wanted to run this, we wanted to
encode the English sentence before doing something like translation into Japanese and if we did that,
um, this maybe we don't need to attend to a whole lot of other things because it's kind of clear
what this means but um, is the way you would translate it would be rather heavily dependent on
the other words in the sentence so you might want to attend to all the other words in this sentence say,
oh, this is is co-, co-, co-, co-occurring with this end example and so if that's the case then
well, we would need to translate it in this way or we need to handle it in this way and that's exactly
the same for you know any other sort of disambiguation, uh, uh, style task so uh, yeah we do something
similar like this so basically cross-attention is attending to a different sequence self-attention is
attending to the same sequence so how do we do this mechanistically in the first place so like let's
say we're translating from Japanese to English um, we would have uh, and we're doing it with an
encoder decoder model where we have already encoded the input sequence and now we're generating
the output sequence with a, for example, a recurrent neural network um, and so if that's the case we have
i, i, hate uh, like this and we want to predict the next word so what we would do is we would take
the current state here and uh, we use something called a query vector and the query vector is essentially
the vector that we want to use to decide what to attend to. We then have key vectors and the key
vectors are the vectors that we would like to use to decide which ones we should be attending to
and then for each query key pair we calculate a weight and we do it like this um, this gear here
is some function that takes in the uh, query vector and the key vector and outputs away.
And notably we use the same function every single time. This is really important again because like
RNN said allows us to extrapolate uh, unlimited length sequences because uh, we only have one set of
you know, we only have one function no matter how long the sequence gets so we can just apply it
over and over and over again. Once we calculate these values we normalize so that they add up to
one using the softmax function and um, basically in this case that would be like 0.76 uh, etc etc.
So step number two is once we have this uh, these attention uh, values here, notably these values
aren't really probabilities, despite the fact that they're between 0 and 1 and they add up to 1
because all we're doing is we're using them to uh, to combine together uh, multiple vectors. So I,
we don't really normally call them attention probabilities or anything like that. I just call them
attention values or normalized attention values um, but once we have these uh, attention, uh, attention
weights we have value vectors and these value vectors are the vectors that we would actually like to
combine together to get the uh, encoding here. And so we take these vectors, we do a weighted
sum of the vectors and get a final uh, final sum here. And we can take this uh, some and use it in
any part of the model that we would like um, and so attention is very broad it can be used in any way.
Now the most common way to do use it is just have lots of self attention players like in uh,
something in a transformer, but um, you can also use it in uh, decoder or other things like that as well.
This is an actual graphical example from the original attention paper. I'm going to give some
